candidates.title: DOGE Used ChatGPT to Cancel Federal Grants at Scale
candidates.description: The New York Times reports that Elon Musk's DOGE agency used ChatGPT to decide which National Endowment for the Humanities grants to cancel en masse. Rather than reviewing funded projects, staffers pulled short summaries and fed them into ChatGPT with the prompt: 'Does the following relate at all to D.E.I.? Respond factually in less than 120 characters. Begin with Yes or No.' The results were sweeping and sometimes bizarre. First confirmed case of a government agency using an AI chatbot to make consequential policy decisions at scale — canceling federal grants based on a simple yes/no AI prompt.
candidates.significance: First documented case of US government using an AI chatbot for mass consequential decisions (grant cancellations). Sets a precedent for AI-driven government policy enforcement with minimal human oversight — exactly the kind of 'AI in governance' inflection point future historians will reference.

candidates.title: OpenAI's Pentagon Deal Exposed as 'Any Lawful Use' Cave
candidates.description: The Verge reports, citing sources familiar with negotiations, that OpenAI's Pentagon deal — which Sam Altman framed as preserving red lines against mass surveillance and lethal autonomy — actually amounts to 'any lawful use.' Every aspect boils down to: if it's technically legal, the US military can use OpenAI's technology. OpenAI's former head of policy research Miles Brundage publicly stated employees should assume 'OpenAI caved + framed it as not caving, and screwed Anthropic while framing it as helping them.' Separately, OpenAI's head of robotics Caitlin Kalinowski resigned over the deal, citing insufficient protections against warrantless surveillance and lethal autonomy.

sources.label: The Verge — How OpenAI caved to the Pentagon

candidates.significance: Reveals the divergence between OpenAI and Anthropic on military AI use — OpenAI quietly accepted 'any lawful use' terms while publicly claiming safety red lines. Combined with the high-profile robotics lead resignation, this crystallizes the split in how frontier AI labs handle government pressure. The 'any lawful use' framing is historically significant because US law has permitted sweeping surveillance programs in the past.

candidates.title: Claude-Powered Intelligence Tools Reportedly Used in US Strike on Iran
candidates.description: Multiple reports indicate Claude-powered intelligence analysis tools played a major role in the US targeted missile strike on Iran that killed Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei. If confirmed, this represents the first known use of frontier AI systems in a targeted military strike that killed a head of state. The irony: the Pentagon is simultaneously trying to ban Anthropic as a supply-chain risk while its military relies on Claude for intelligence operations.

sources.label: The Verge (mentions Claude role in Iran strike)

candidates.significance: If confirmed, this is a watershed moment: frontier AI directly contributing to a military operation that killed a head of state. It also reveals the deep contradiction in the Pentagon's Anthropic policy — banning the company whose AI tools the military actively depends on for intelligence. This would be one of the most consequential real-world applications of AI in history.

questionUpdates.newEvidence: Three major developments in rapid succession (Mar 5-7): (1) Claude-powered intelligence tools reportedly played a key role in the US strike on Iran that killed Khamenei, revealing the Pentagon's deep operational dependence on the very AI it designated a supply-chain risk. (2) OpenAI's Pentagon deal exposed as 'any lawful use' — OpenAI caved on surveillance/lethal autonomy red lines while framing it as standing firm, directly undermining Anthropic's negotiating position. (3) OpenAI's head of robotics Caitlin Kalinowski resigned in protest, and former policy head Miles Brundage publicly accused OpenAI of caving. The situation has evolved from a bilateral Pentagon-Anthropic standoff into a broader industry fracture: OpenAI chose compliance, Anthropic chose resistance, and the Pentagon may be forced to acknowledge its own operational dependence on Claude.
